labs.clinical_note: White blood cell count is normal.
Red blood cell count is normal.
Red blood cell size is normal.
Hemoglobin content per red cell is normal.
Red cell size variation is normal.
Magnesium is at the lower end of normal. Alcohol intake can deplete magnesium stores.
Fasting glucose is within normal limits.
Albumin is normal, indicating preserved liver synthetic function.
ALT is elevated above normal. This liver enzyme rises when liver cells are under stress or experiencing damage.
AST is elevated. The pattern of ALT being higher than AST is characteristic of metabolic liver strain rather than alcohol-related damage.
Total cholesterol remains elevated despite statin therapy.
LDL cholesterol is above optimal despite statin medication.
HDL is at the lower threshold of normal.
Triglycerides are elevated. Alcohol consumption and refined carbohydrates are common contributors.
GGT is elevated, particularly sensitive to alcohol intake.
Ferritin is in the upper portion of normal.
Total testosterone is below normal range for adult males.
Estradiol is elevated above the optimal range for males.

diagnoses.diagnosis: Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD) with Alcohol-Related Component
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Elevated liver enzymes with ALT (68) > AST (52) pattern is characteristic of metabolic fatty liver. GGT elevation to 88 U/L suggests alcohol contribution given regular use (3-5x/week). Elevated triglycerides (192 mg/dL) and upper-normal ferritin (320 ng/mL) support hepatic fat accumulation. Family history of liver cirrhosis increases concern. Negative hepatitis serology rules out viral etiology.

Despite Atorvastatin 20mg, lipid panel shows persistent dyslipidemia: LDL 128 mg/dL, triglycerides 192 mg/dL, HDL 42 mg/dL. Non-HDL cholesterol 176 mg/dL and elevated hs-CRP (2.4 mg/L) indicate residual cardiovascular risk. Family history of heart attack compounds risk.

Total testosterone (245 ng/dL) and free testosterone (7.8 pg/mL) below reference. Normal LH/FSH suggests secondary suppression. Elevated estradiol (48 pg/mL) indicates increased aromatization, commonly seen with body fat, alcohol, and liver dysfunction. Low DHEA-S (95 µg/dL) reflects adrenal androgen decline.

foods_to_eat.food: Cruciferous vegetables — broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts (brócoli, coliflor, col de Bruselas)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Contains DIM and glucosinolates that support liver detoxification and help metabolize excess estrogen.

foods_to_eat.food: Fatty fish — salmon, sardines, mackerel (salmón, sardinas, caballa)
foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 4-5 servings per week, 4-6 oz per serving
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: EPA and DHA omega-3s lower triglycerides and reduce inflammation. Also help decrease liver fat.
Consistently shown to reduce liver enzyme levels and decrease liver fibrosis risk.

foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1/2 cup cooked or raw, 4-5 times per week
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Rich in betaine, which helps the liver process and export fat.
Monounsaturated fats raise HDL cholesterol. Contains compounds that protect liver cells.
High in zinc, essential for testosterone production and aromatase inhibition.

foods_to_eat.food: Eggs from pasture-raised hens (huevos de gallinas libres)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Excellent source of choline, critical for liver fat export.

foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1/2 cup seeds or 4 oz unsweetened juice, 4-5 times per week
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Contains compounds that inhibit aromatase, reducing testosterone-to-estrogen conversion.
Contains cynarin, which stimulates bile production and supports liver regeneration.
Allicin supports healthy cholesterol and liver detoxification.

foods_to_eat.food: Turmeric with black pepper (cúrcuma con pimienta negra)
foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1 teaspoon turmeric with black pepper daily
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Curcumin reduces liver inflammation and supports glutathione production.
Omega-3 ALA and polyphenols improve cholesterol balance and benefit liver fat.

foods_to_eat.food: Extra virgin olive oil (aceite de oliva extra virgen)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Oleic acid and polyphenols reduce liver inflammation.
Highest dietary source of zinc for testosterone synthesis.

foods_to_eat.food: Black beans and lentils (frijoles negros, lentejas)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Soluble fiber lowers LDL and triglycerides.

foods_to_avoid.food: Alcohol — beer, wine, spirits (cerveza, vino, licores)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Alcohol is directly toxic to liver cells. Your elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST, GGT) indicate strain. Complete elimination for 3-6 months is essential.
Fructose is processed by the liver and directly converted to fat.
Creates inflammatory compounds that damage liver cells.
High in saturated fat and preservatives that increase cardiovascular risk.

foods_to_avoid.food: Refined carbohydrates (pan blanco, arroz blanco, pasteles)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Rapidly converted to glucose, which the liver converts to fat.

foods_to_avoid.food: Soy products in excess (productos de soya)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Contains phytoestrogens that can add to estrogen load.
Fructose is directly converted to liver fat.

foods_to_avoid.food: Vegetable oils high in omega-6 (aceite de maíz, soya, girasol)

foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: No more than 2 servings per week
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: High intake associated with fatty liver progression.
Combination of refined flour, sugar, and poor-quality fats burdens the liver.

exercise_recommendations.exercise_type: Zone 2 Cardiovascular — swimming, brisk walking, cycling
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Sustained moderate cardio reduces liver fat by 30-40% after 12-16 weeks. You already swim, so increasing frequency builds on your routine.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: Blood pressure managed with Amlodipine. Swimming is low-impact and excellent choice.

exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Heavy compound training is most effective for raising testosterone naturally. Building muscle helps metabolize excess estrogen.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: Avoid breath-holding during lifts. Breathe out on exertion.

exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Walking after meals reduces triglyceride spikes by helping muscles absorb nutrients.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Most well-studied liver-protective compound. Reduces ALT/AST, protects liver cells, supports glutathione. Your elevated enzymes indicate clear benefit.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interaction with Atorvastatin or Amlodipine.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Cardo mariano is widely available in Latin American pharmacies.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Therapeutic doses reduce triglycerides 20-30%. Your triglycerides at 192 mg/dL remain elevated despite statin. Also reduces liver fat and inflammation.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Widely available in Latin American pharmacies.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Precursor to glutathione, liver's master antioxidant. Reduces ALT/AST in fatty liver. Important given alcohol history.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No medication interactions. Take with food.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: NAC (N-Acetilcisteína) available in most Latin American pharmacies.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Rate-limiting for testosterone synthesis. Inhibits aromatase. Alcohol depletes zinc stores.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: Take 2+ hours apart from medications. Do not exceed 50 mg/day.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Available in health food stores. Zinc Gluconate common alternative.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Supports healthy estrogen metabolism. Your estradiol at 48 pg/mL is above optimal for males.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Available in health food stores in major cities.

patient_summary: Your lab panel reviewed 47 markers across blood, liver, cardiovascular, and hormonal systems, with 9 values outside normal range. The primary area requiring attention is your liver, where elevated enzymes (ALT 68 U/L, AST 52 U/L, GGT 88 U/L) indicate strain — this pattern combined with elevated triglycerides points to fat accumulation that warrants intervention. Your cardiovascular markers show persistent lipid imbalances despite statin therapy, with elevated triglycerides (192 mg/dL) and borderline HDL (42 mg/dL), accompanied by elevated hs-CRP (2.4 mg/L). Your hormonal panel reveals testosterone levels below optimal (total 245 ng/dL, free 7.8 pg/mL) with elevated estradiol (48 pg/mL), a pattern influenced by liver function and lifestyle factors. The most impactful first steps are complete elimination of alcohol for 3-6 months, adding milk thistle (silymarin) and omega-3 fish oil daily, and increasing cardiovascular exercise to 200+ minutes weekly. Repeat testing at the 3-month mark is recommended to track progress.

system_summaries.summary: All complete blood count markers are within normal range with no signs of anemia or immune abnormalities.
Kidney function is normal (creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, BUN 18 mg/dL). Magnesium at 1.75 mg/dL is at lower threshold — alcohol can deplete magnesium stores.

Your liver shows signs of strain with elevated ALT (68 U/L), AST (52 U/L), and GGT (88 U/L). The ALT > AST pattern suggests metabolic fatty accumulation, while elevated GGT reflects alcohol-related stress. Hepatitis B and C are negative. Complete alcohol elimination, dietary changes, and liver-supportive supplements are the primary interventions.
Blood sugar regulation is normal with fasting glucose at 95 mg/dL and HbA1c at 5.5%. Maintain a diet low in refined carbohydrates.

Despite Atorvastatin therapy, your lipid panel shows residual risk: LDL (128 mg/dL) and triglycerides (192 mg/dL) remain elevated while HDL (42 mg/dL) is borderline. Triglyceride elevation is linked to alcohol intake. Omega-3 supplementation and dietary changes will complement your statin.
Your hs-CRP at 2.4 mg/L indicates elevated systemic inflammation, an independent cardiovascular risk factor. Reducing alcohol, increasing omega-3 intake, and exercise will help lower this marker.

Testosterone levels are below optimal (total 245 ng/dL, free 7.8 pg/mL) while estradiol is elevated at 48 pg/mL. This indicates increased testosterone-to-estrogen conversion, commonly seen with body fat, alcohol, and liver strain. Reducing alcohol, resistance training, and zinc supplementation will support natural testosterone production.
